heading: Learn by Watching
body:
You can also watch the following videos to
learn more about MongoDB Search:

Watch an overview of Atlas and MongoDB Search and get started setting up
MongoDB Search for your data. The video demonstrates how to load sample data on
your cluster, create a MongoDB Search index, and run a sample query using
Search Tester and Data Explorer.

*Duration: 10 Minutes*

[https://youtu.be/HsS0z3eOCSQ](https://youtu.be/HsS0z3eOCSQ)

Follow along with the following video to learn how to configure your
MongoDB Search index and run queries from your application.

*Duration: 7 Minutes*

[https://youtu.be/3fRCD\_QptVQ](https://youtu.be/3fRCD_QptVQ)

Follow along with the following video tutorial walk-through that
demonstrates how to build MongoDB Search queries for a Restaurant Finder demo
application, which is also available at [www.atlassearchrestaurants.com](https://www.atlassearchrestaurants.com/).

*Duration: 20 Minutes*

[https://youtu.be/s2kXYZRE7pA](https://youtu.be/s2kXYZRE7pA)

MongoDB Search is an embedded full-text search feature
that gives you a seamless, scalable experience for building
relevance-based app features. This quick start describes
how to get started in the following steps:

- Create a MongoDB Search index on a sample collection.

- Build a MongoDB Search query to search the collection.
  You will learn how to:

  - Run a basic query to search for a term in a field

  - Use an operator to refine your search

  - Add a search option to process your results

*Time required: 15 minutes*

> **Note:** This tutorial includes examples for a selection of clients.
> For all supported clients, see the
> [MongoDB Search indexes](fts-manage-indexes) and
> [Queries](ref-query-management) pages.

a MongoDB Search index is a data structure that categorizes data in an easily
searchable format. It maps terms with documents that
contain those terms to enable fast retrieval of documents
at query-time.

You must configure a MongoDB Search index to query data using MongoDB Search.
We recommend that you index the fields that you regularly
use to sort or filter your data.

To learn more, see [MongoDB Search indexes](fts-manage-indexes).

In this section, you create a MongoDB Search index on the sample
[movies](mflix-movies) collection. You can create the
MongoDB Search index in either an Atlas cluster or a
deployment hosted on your local computer.

Atlas Cluster

- Install the MongoDB Python Driver.

  ```sh
  pip install pymongo
  ```

  For detailed installation instructions, see
  [MongoDB Python Driver documentation](languages/python/pymongo-driver/get-started).

- Define the index.

  Paste the following code into a file named `create_index.py`.

  ```python
  from pymongo import MongoClient
  from pymongo.operations import SearchIndexModel

  # connect to your Atlas deployment
  uri = "<connection-string>"
  client = MongoClient(uri)

  # set namespace
  database = client["sample_mflix"]
  collection = database["movies"]

  # define your MongoDB Search index
  search_index_model = SearchIndexModel(
      definition={
          "mappings": {
              "dynamic": True
          },
      },
      name="default",
  )

  # create the index
  result = collection.create_search_index(model=search_index_model)
  print(result)

  ```

  Atlas Cluster

  Replace `<connection-string>` with the connection string for your
  Atlas cluster or local Atlas deployment.

  Atlas Cluster

  Your connection string should use the following format:

  ```
  mongodb+srv://<db_username>:<db_password>@<clusterName>.<hostname>.mongodb.net
  ```

  To learn more, see [Driver](connect-via-driver).

  Local or Self-Managed

  Your connection string should use the
  following format:

  ```
  mongodb://localhost:<port-number>/?directConnection=true
  ```

  To learn more, see [Connection Strings](manual/reference/connection-string).

  IMPORTANT: Ensure that your connection string includes your database
  user's credentials. To learn more about finding your connection string,
  see [Driver](connect-via-driver).

  Local Deployment

  Your connection string should use the following format:

  ```
  mongodb://localhost:<port-number>/?directConnection=true
  ```

- Create the index.

  **Input:**

  ```shell
  python create_index.py
  ```

  **Output:**

  ```
  default
  ```

Local Deployment

- Install the MongoDB Python Driver.

  ```sh
  pip install pymongo
  ```

  For detailed installation instructions, see
  [MongoDB Python Driver documentation](languages/python/pymongo-driver/get-started).

- Define the index.

  Paste the following code into a file named `create_index.py`.

  ```python
  from pymongo import MongoClient
  from pymongo.operations import SearchIndexModel

  # connect to your Atlas deployment
  uri = "<connection-string>"
  client = MongoClient(uri)

  # set namespace
  database = client["sample_mflix"]
  collection = database["movies"]

  # define your MongoDB Search index
  search_index_model = SearchIndexModel(
      definition={
          "mappings": {
              "dynamic": True
          },
      },
      name="default",
  )

  # create the index
  result = collection.create_search_index(model=search_index_model)
  print(result)

  ```

  Atlas Cluster

  Replace `<connection-string>` with the connection string for your
  Atlas cluster or local Atlas deployment.

  Atlas Cluster

  Your connection string should use the following format:

  ```
  mongodb+srv://<db_username>:<db_password>@<clusterName>.<hostname>.mongodb.net
  ```

  To learn more, see [Driver](connect-via-driver).

  Local or Self-Managed

  Your connection string should use the
  following format:

  ```
  mongodb://localhost:<port-number>/?directConnection=true
  ```

  To learn more, see [Connection Strings](manual/reference/connection-string).

  IMPORTANT: Ensure that your connection string includes your database
  user's credentials. To learn more about finding your connection string,
  see [Driver](connect-via-driver).

  Local Deployment

  Your connection string should use the following format:

  ```
  mongodb://localhost:<port-number>/?directConnection=true
  ```

- Create the index.

  **Input:**

  ```shell
  python create_index.py
  ```

  **Output:**

  ```
  default
  ```

MongoDB Search queries take the form of an [aggregation pipeline stage](manual/aggregation). You use MongoDB Search primarily with the [$search](pipe.$search) stage,
which must be the first stage in the query pipeline. You can also use
this stage in conjunction with other stages in your pipeline.

When you run a MongoDB Search query, MongoDB Search uses the search index to
locate and retrieve relevant data from the collection.
MongoDB Search also provides the [$searchMeta](pipe.$searchMeta) stage,
multiple sub-pipelines, and several query
[MongoDB Search operators](operators-ref) and [collectors](collectors-ref)
that you can use to further refine your search results.

To learn more, see [Queries and Indexes](fts-about-queries).

In this section, you run queries against the indexed collection.

Run a basic `$search` query by using the
[text](text-ref) operator.

- Create a new file named `run_query.py` and paste the following code.

  This query has the following search criteria:

  - The `plot` field must contain the word `baseball`.

  - [$limit](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/limit) and
    [$project](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/project) stages
    to return only 3 documents and the `title` and `plot` fields.

  ```python
  import pymongo

  client = pymongo.MongoClient('<connection-string>')
  result = client['sample_mflix']['movies'].aggregate([
    {
      '$search': 
       {
          'text': {
            'query': 'baseball',
            'path': 'plot'
          }
       }
    }, 
    {
      '$limit': 3
    }, 
    {
      '$project': {
         '_id': 0,
         'title': 1,
         'plot': 1
      }
    }
  ])

  for i in result:
      print(i)

  ```

- Specify the `<connection-string>`, then run the query:

  **Input:**

  ```bash
  python run_query.py
  ```

  **Output:**

  ```shell
  {'plot': 'A trio of guys try and make up for missed opportunities in childhood by forming a three-player baseball team to compete against standard children baseball squads.', 'title': 'The Benchwarmers'}
  {'plot': 'A young boy is bequeathed the ownership of a professional baseball team.', 'title': 'Little Big League'}
  {'plot': 'A trained chimpanzee plays third base for a minor-league baseball team.', 'title': 'Ed'}
  ```

MongoDB Search provides several [operators](fts-operators) that you
can use to build complex queries and retrieve more specific
results for your use case. Update your `$search` query to use the
[compound](compound-ref) operator to combine
multiple operators into a single query.

- Modify `run_query.py` to use the compound query.

  This query has the following search criteria:

  - The `plot` field must contain the word `baseball`.

  - The `genres` field must not contain either `Comedy` or
    `Romance`.

  - [$limit](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/limit) and
    [$project](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/project) stages
    to return only 3 documents and the `title`, `plot`, and
    `genres` fields.

  ```python
  import pymongo

  client = pymongo.MongoClient('<connection-string>')
  result = client['sample_mflix']['movies'].aggregate([
    {
      '$search': {
        'compound': {
          'must': [
            {
              'text': {
                'query': 'baseball',
                'path': 'plot'
              }
            }
          ],
          'mustNot': [
            {
              'text': {
                'query': ['Comedy', 'Romance'],
                'path': 'genres'
              }
            }
          ]
        }
      }
    }, 
    {
      '$limit': 3
    },
    {
      '$project': {
        '_id': 0,           
        'title': 1,
        'plot': 1,
        'genres': 1
      }
    }
  ])

  for i in result:
      print(i)

  ```

- Specify the `<connection-string>`, then run the query:

  **Input:**

  ```bash
  python run_query.py
  ```

  **Output:**

  ```shell
  {'plot': 'The story of the life and career of the famed baseball player, Lou Gehrig.', 'genres': ['Biography', 'Drama', 'Family'], 'title': 'The Pride of the Yankees'}
  {'plot': 'Babe Ruth becomes a baseball legend but is unheroic to those who know him.', 'genres': ['Biography', 'Drama', 'Sport'], 'title': 'The Babe'}
  {'plot': 'Dominican baseball star Miguel "Sugar" Santos is recruited to play in the U.S. minor-leagues.', 'genres': ['Drama', 'Sport'], 'title': 'Sugar'}
  ```

MongoDB Search provides several [search options](fts-search-options)
that you can use to further process your MongoDB Search query results.
Add the [sorting](sort-ref) option to your query to
display the results in a specific order.

- Modify `run_query.py` to add the [sorting](sort-ref) option.

  This query has the following search criteria:

  - The `plot` field must contain the word `baseball`.

  - The `genres` field must not contain either `Comedy` or
    `Romance`.

  - Sort the results by the `released` date
    field in descending order.
    MongoDB Search returns the most recent movies first.

  - [$limit](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/limit) and
    [$project](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/project) stages
    to return only 3 documents and the `title`, `plot`,
    `genres`, and `released` fields.

  ```python
  import pymongo

  client = pymongo.MongoClient('<connection-string>')
  result = client['sample_mflix']['movies'].aggregate([
    {
      '$search': {
        'compound': {
          'must': [
            {
              'text': {
                'query': 'baseball',
                'path': 'plot'
              }
            }
          ],
          'mustNot': [
            {
              'text': {
                'query': ['Comedy', 'Romance'],
                'path': 'genres'
              }
            }
          ]
        },
        'sort': {
            'released': -1
        }
      }
    }, 
    {
      '$limit': 3
    },
    {
      '$project': {
        '_id': 0,           
        'title': 1,
        'plot': 1,
        'genres': 1,
        'released': 1
      }
    }
  ])

  for i in result:
      print(i)

  ```

- Specify the `<connection-string>`, then run the query:

  **Input:**

  ```bash
  python run_query.py
  ```

  **Output:**

  ```shell
  {'plot': 'A sports agent stages an unconventional recruitment strategy to get talented Indian cricket players to play Major League Baseball.', 'genres': ['Biography', 'Drama', 'Sport'], 'title': 'Million Dollar Arm', 'released': datetime.datetime(2014, 5, 16, 0, 0)}
  {'plot': 'A Taiwanese high school baseball team travels to Japan in 1931 to compete in a national tournament.', 'genres': ['Biography', 'Drama', 'History'], 'title': 'Kano', 'released': datetime.datetime(2014, 2, 27, 0, 0)}
  {'plot': "12-year-old Josh is a mixed race boy and a promising baseball player...", 'genres': ['Drama'], 'title': 'Calloused Hands', 'released': datetime.datetime(2013, 3, 3, 0, 0)}
  ```

from pymongo import MongoClient
from pymongo.operations import SearchIndexModel

# connect to your Atlas deployment
uri = "<connection-string>"
client = MongoClient(uri)

# set namespace
database = client["sample_mflix"]
collection = database["movies"]

# define your MongoDB Search index
search_index_model = SearchIndexModel(
    definition={
        "mappings": {
            "dynamic": True
        },
    },
    name="default",
)

# create the index
result = collection.create_search_index(model=search_index_model)
print(result)

# connect to your Atlas deployment

# set namespace

# create the index
result = collection.create_search_index(model=search_index_model)
print(result)
